mark a circle around junction of dop and stone.

Designed by Jim Perkins, Copyright May 23, 2003
Average face up brilliance ISO=89.5% COS=78.1% At 10 degrees tilt ISO= 89.2%
COS= 78.6%. At 20 degrees tilt ISO= 75.9% COS= 63.9%.
comments:
The 72-index teeth are 5 deg. apart. Therefore, designing with adjacent indices isn't as much of a
problem as it would be for finer resolution index gears. I have tried to optimize this design for
CREATIVE GEMS SC-33 Kashmir Blue Synthetic flame-fusion corundum. I designed it for an 8mm ring stone.
It should work nice in most light to medium material RI 1.7+ in sizes 8mm and larger.

name: PC02189.ASC
header:
PC 02.189 FANTASIA
Designed By:Jim Perkins 7/24/01
Average light return face up, 82.0%
comments:
Start by cutting a through d. Then cut g1,g4.
Next cut g2,g3. Then follow in order e through j.
Cut the crown in order given 1 through 11, polish.
Then cut table and polish.

When I started designing this I had something completely
different in mind. When this started to evolve I didn't
fight it. I just worked with my minds eye and my concious mind
and let them work it out. It maybe one of my best ovals yet!

July 2015

name: PC02236A.ASC
header:
PC 02.236A The Rebecca Oval, Optimized
for easier cutting
Steven Weintraub Jr.
3/26/2019
comments:
The Rebecca Oval was created for a dear friend of mine named Rebecca Burier who passed away last year.
She was an incredible woman and this design is a tribute to her greatness. The design is for larger,
shallow stone and was optimized for lower-R.I. stone like Quartz. This design does not look great in
GemRay but in real life it makes one of the most beautiful and captivating stones I have ever seen.

header:
PC 02.260 Soulmate Oval 5x6
Verhesen Wouter; Unpublished
comments:
Set L/W 1.2 at reference lines 48-96 and 24-72 with respect to T1. At the indicated angle,
"e" is meetpoint with the girdle and "w1", after which the other barion facets will become
meetpoint and level the girdle as well. If w1-w4 is set first, "a-e" angles must be adjusted
accordingly, or vice versa. R.I.: 1.58 - 1.81

name: PC04213.ASC
header:
PC 04.213 THE EMERALD ISLE CUT
Designed by Jim Perkins
Copyright July 7, 2002
Average face up brilliance ISO=88.5% COS=80.6% RI=1.54
comments:
A classic looking emerald cut in this length/width is
an essential. Here it is in an RI for quartz and beryl.
However it is easy enough to optimize it for materials
of higher IR using the tangent/ratio formula.

name: PC04215.ASC
header:
PC 04.215 INCA EMERALD
Designed by Jim Perkins
Copyright December 7, 2003
comments:
This is a pretty traditonal antique looking emerald cut. I had a particular project for some stones
like this cut in emeralds which were a little dark. However, these angles will work well for such a
stone. Computed average brilliance for emerald. ISO= 71.3% COS= 56.7% DELTA=14.6% Dispersion= 13.1%
Scintillation= 32.8%. You can also use this design for RI>1.58 with no angle changes.

June 2020, Pers. Comm. AW to RWS 2016.

angle to meet 4-4-8-8
See other L/W variants. Pavilion angles don't change with L/W.
For larger corners, adjust angle of steps 5 and B.

name: PC07160.ASC
header:
PC 07.160 Meetpoint Radiant Square Classic
Reconstructed by Roland Arajs from top and bottom views on
whiteflash.com/about-diamonds/diamond-education/the-radiant-cut-diamond.htm
I make no claim that the design is the same as the original by Henry Grossbard.
comments:
Some people claim that step-facet designs are not meetpoint designs and are
therefore not reproducible. I find such statements a bit puzzling because
most, if not all, such designs can be created using a meetpoint sequence by
cutting temporary facets, as shown by PF3 in the cutting sequence for this design.

round. Reflection pattern similar to round brilliant. Warning: if using this
commercially, this might be too similar to design patent US D639202 S1 and
utility patent US20120297826 A1.

Pavilion depth from lower girdle line to culet facet should measure 8.69 mm for total depth to be
reasonable. Replicas vary in color, weight Not shown on this diagram are extra girdle facets, two

uncommon 'Stairwell' pavilion by the great Walt Heitland. It reminds me of a Japanese Zen sand

3/26/2019 to ease cutting.

header:
PC 10.059 Vortex Tri-Heart
by Roland Arajs. Copyright May, 2014
comments:
Facets G,H, on crown use what I call 3-sub-1 radial symmetry; so-called because they are created
in GemCad using 3-fold radial symmetry and then deleting the facets at the tip of the heart. Also,
facet D is 18-50-82 sub 50.

name: PC10060.ASC
header:
PC 10.060 Trilliant Heart
Robert W. Strickland 15 July 2014
For gemcad.blogspot.com
comments:
This design is basically a trilliant with two of the corners rounded off
and one bulging side flattened. Much of it can be cut with 3-fold,
mirror-image symmetry. Angles work as-is for RI from 1.54 to 2.13.
The copyright for this work is assigned to the public domain.

name: PC10061.ASC
header:
PC 10.061 Cube Illusion Heart
by Robert W. Strickland 28 Aug 2014
Copyright assigned to public domain
comments:
Sid Word was the master of the checkerboard. He discovered that what he
termed a "mono" pavilion was all that was needed with a checker crown. I
began with my Cube Illusion Triangle 13.110, rounded off two corners and
added a flat-side girdle facet, which is not level. Angles OK for any RI.

name: PC10062.ASC
header:
PC 10.062 Heart of Texas
by Robert W. Strickland 11 Aug 2014
Copyright assigned to the public domain.
comments:
The step 1 facets make the ECED part of the girdle outline. They
don't have to meet except at the tip of the heart.
The step 2 facets at 45 deg. reflect the viewer and make a dark field for
the bright star. Don't change the pavilion angles.

name: PC10063.ASC
header:
PC 10.063 Triple-Reflex Vortex TriHeart
Designed by Roland Arajs, 2014
comments:
The high pavilion angles are for triple reflection rather than the standard double.
This dramatically increases tilt performance in low-RI stones.

name: PC10064.ASC
header:
PC 10.064 Another Nine Main Heart
by Robert W. Strickland, 30 July 2015
Copyright assigned to the public domain
comments:
Steps 1-5 meet at a temporary centerpoint.
Crown mains all at same angle.
Girdle facet 10 not level.

DESIGNED BY JIM PERKINS
COPYRIGHT JULY 5, 2002
Average Faceup brilliance ISO=76.1% COS=57.5% @RI=1.63
comments:
Here's an easy and fast cutting design that should work without
for most materials without angle changes.I had tourmaline in mind
though. I would T/R pavilion angles for higher refractive material
depending on color saturation. Enjoy it!

name: PC13176.ASC
header:
PC 13.176 Tritetra
by Robert W. Strickland 4/10/2006
comments:
This cut is a tetrahedron with bulging sides divided into triangles. It has
four nearly identical faces. Any of them can be the "top," and any apex can
be the "bottom." Use the same angles for any material. Step 2 will determine
the size. From the culet to the meet point of 1-1-2-2 is 0.157W.

"bottom."
Copyright assigned to the public domain.

header:
PC 13.178 Tritetra Small
by Robert W. Strickland 4/26/06, revised 3/22/07
TFG Newsletter V30n4 Oct 2009 p14
Copyright assigned to the public domain.
comments:
This cut is a tetrahedron with bulging sides divided into triangles. It has
four nearly identical faces. Any of them can be the "top," and any apex can
be the "bottom." Use the same angles for any material. Step 2 will determine
the size. From the culet to the meet point of 1-1-2-2 is 0.107 W.

name: PC21228.ASC
header:
PC 21.228 Will's Sparkler with Table
Error cutting Graham's Easy 8
Modified by Will Smith
comments:
This gives a stone with lots of color and flash. You decide the size of table. Do not get it very large.
You decide if you want P2 to go to girdle. You get good flass either way.
You decide how deep to cut C3 into C1 & C2. It will be correct if you do not chenge height for C2,
change angle and index and cut to hard stop to get C3. Beautiful error.

Designed by Jim Perkins
Copyright May 28, 2005.
Cut in corundum, CZ, garnet, topaz, tourmaline, spinel, yag or zircon.
comments:
Computed performance will vary depending on specific characteristics of your rough, quality of cut and polish
and other factors which are unpredictable by the designer. Computed performance using GemRayX @ 120
pixel resolution and 10* head shadow half-angle. ISO=81.1% COS=70.9% DISPERSION=6.3%
SCINTILLATION=35.3%. 95.3% REFLECTED BRILLIANCE IF HEAD SHADOW IS NOT CONSIDERED.

comments:
